context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 26th February, 2024
Days Covered: 04 and 05 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Sorting by Reversals [15 points]

We have a permutation of the numbers {0, 1, ..., n − 1}, and we'd like to manipulate it so it becomes sorted in increasing order. For this problem, we're only allowed to make reversal moves: from a list (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}), we can pick a pair of indices i < j and reverse the entire subsequence from a_i to a_j:
    (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_i, a_{i+1}, ..., a_{j−1}, a_j, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1})
  → (a0, ..., a_{i−1}, a_j, a_{j−1}, ..., a_{i+1}, a_i, a_{j+1}, ..., a_{n−1}).
If (i, j) is an inverted pair (i.e., i < j but a_i > a_j), call this move a good reversal.

In this problem we'll show that if we start with any permutation (x0, ..., x_{n−1}) of {0, ..., n − 1} and keep making good reversal moves, then no matter which ones we choose, we will eventually terminate with a sorted sequence.

(a) [3 pts] Describe this scenario as a state machine, where the states are the permutations of {0, 1, ..., n − 1}. What are the transitions? The start state?
(b) [3 pts] What are the final states? How do you know? Conclude partial correctness: if an execution of the state machine terminates, then the list must be sorted.
(c) [6 pts] In lecture, we proved termination of swap-sort by proving that the number of inverted pairs strictly decreases as we make swaps. This is not true for this problem! For example, (9, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0) can transition to (0, 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 9), but the former has 17 inverted pairs while the latter has 28 inverted pairs.
Even so, please find a derived variable for this problem that (i) has only nonnegative integer values and (ii) is strictly decreasing. Be sure to prove that your derived variable satisfies both properties.
    Hint: Think of (a0, ..., a_{n−1}) as digits of a base-n number.
(d) [3 pts] Use parts (b) and (c) to prove total correctness: every execution must eventually terminate with a fully sorted list.


Problem 2. A Fibonacci Sum [10 points]

Recall that the Fibonacci sequence is given by F0 = 0, F1 = 1, and Fn = F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for n > 1, so the sequence starts with 0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ....

(a) [8 pts] Use the perturbation method to find the closed form of the sum
    S = Σ_{n=1}^∞ Fn / c^n,
where c = 10^5. You may assume the sum converges.
    Hint: Try comparing S with S/c.
(b) [2 pts] Verify your answer with WolframAlpha (www.wolframalpha.com) or some other high-precision calculator, recalling that c = 10^5. What are the first 50 digits of your result? What is the pattern and does it make sense?


Problem 3. Integral Method [15 points]

Let f(x) := x / (5 + x)^3, and let S_n := Σ_{x=n}^∞ f(x). In this problem, we will use the integral method to estimate the infinite sum S0 to an accuracy of 1/200. In other words, we will find bounds u and v such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
Feel free to use a calculator (e.g., WolframAlpha) for algebraic/numerical computations, but be sure to justify all of your reasoning.

(a) [2 pts] Explain briefly why applying the integral method directly to S0 will not work.
(b) [3 pts] Find the smallest index i such that the sum S_i can be approximated by applying the integral method directly.
(c) [4 pts] Use your answer from the previous part to find upper and lower bounds for S0. What accuracy does your approximation achieve? (I.e., what is the difference between your upper and lower bounds?)
(d) [6 pts] Find the smallest index j such that the sum S_j can be approximated to an accuracy of 1/200 by applying the integral method directly. Use your answer to find u and v (expressed as rational numbers) such that u ≤ S0 ≤ v and |v − u| ≤ 1/200.
    Hint: j should be a single digit integer.


Problem 4. Some Sums [10 points]

Find the closed form of each of the summations below.

(a) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=0}^n (5^i − 7^i) / 13^i
(b) [5 pts]  Σ_{i=1}^n Σ_{j=1}^k (3^j − i)

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on Problem 1, especially part (c). I don’t really get what a “derived variable” is here or why the hint about base-\(n\) numbers helps.
exchanges.tutor:
Good question to start with. A derived variable is just a function of the state — a number you compute from the current permutation — that you can track as the machine runs. The goal is to find one that strictly decreases with every good reversal move.

Now, the hint says to think of (a0, a1, ..., a_{n−1}) as the digits of a base-n number. If you did that, what single number would the sorted permutation (0, 1, 2, ..., n−1) represent, and what number would the 'largest' permutation represent? Which one is bigger?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking about a core concept (derived variable) and the hint. I should clarify what a derived variable is briefly, then use a Socratic question to guide them toward the base-n hint without giving anything away. Keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I think the sorted one would be the base-\(n\) number with digits \(0,1,2,\dots,n-1\), but I’m not sure how that helps with reversals. I also don’t know what you mean by “largest” here—largest as a permutation or largest as the base-\(n\) value?
exchanges.tutor:
By 'largest' I mean largest as a base-n number — so the permutation whose digits, read left to right, give the biggest base-n value.

Here's the key question: when you make a good reversal (swapping a segment where a_i > a_j with i < j), does the base-n value of the permutation go up or down? Try a small example, say n=3, and see what happens.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress but needs a nudge to connect the base-n value to the direction of good reversals. I should clarify 'largest' briefly, then ask them to think about what a good reversal does to the base-n value — does it increase or decrease it? This moves directly toward the solution without giving it away.

exchanges.student: I tried a tiny one and it seems like it goes down, but I don’t see why that has to always happen. Like I can believe the leftmost changed digit matters, but then a whole block gets reversed and that part gets messy for me. Could you just explain the general reason more directly?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't explain it directly, but you're already close! You said the leftmost changed digit matters — that's exactly the right instinct. 

Focus just on position i: before the reversal, the digit there is a_i. After, it becomes a_j. Given that this is a *good* reversal, what do you know about how a_i and a_j compare? And since position i is the leftmost changed position, what does that tell you about the overall base-n value?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the answer directly, which I should refuse. However, they are genuinely struggling, so I should guide them more specifically toward the key insight without giving it away. The key is that in a good reversal, a_i > a_j, so replacing a_i with a_j at position i (the leftmost changed position) strictly decreases the base-n value, regardless of what happens to the rest of the block. I'll ask a targeted Socratic question about just the leftmost changed digit.

exchanges.student: I guess since it’s a good reversal, \(a_i > a_j\), so the digit at the first changed position gets smaller. So that should make the whole base-\(n\) number smaller no matter what happens later. I’m still fuzzy on how to turn that into an actual proof for part (c), and also what exactly I’m supposed to state as the derived variable.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right — that's the core of the argument. The leftmost changed position dominates in base-n comparison, so the whole value drops.

So: can you now state the derived variable explicitly as a formula? And once you have that, what two things do you need to prove about it to satisfy the problem's requirements (i) and (ii)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the core insight: the leftmost changed digit decreases, which dominates all later positions in base-n comparison. They now need to (1) formally state the derived variable and (2) structure the proof. I should affirm their insight, prompt them to state the derived variable explicitly, and then ask them to think about how to formalize the argument they just made. I should not write the proof for them.

exchanges.student: I think the derived variable is something like \(D(a_0,\dots,a_{n-1})=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} a_k n^{n-1-k}\), treating the permutation as a base-\(n\) number. But I’m not sure how formal they want the “nonnegative integer values” part, because that seems almost too obvious, and I’m worried I’m missing something.
exchanges.tutor:
That's exactly the right derived variable. And you're right that property (i) is straightforward — just briefly justify why D is always a nonneg integer (hint: what are the a_k values?). A sentence is enough.

The real work is property (ii). You already have the key idea: the leftmost changed position has a strictly smaller digit after the reversal. Can you now try to write that argument more carefully? What specifically happens to D at position i versus all the positions after it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the derived variable. Their concern about property (i) being 'too obvious' is worth addressing briefly — it's fine to state it concisely, as it's indeed straightforward. I should affirm this and then push them toward actually writing the proof for property (ii), which is the harder and more important part.

exchanges.student: I’m getting tangled trying to write it cleanly. I want to say “the earlier digit matters more,” but that feels informal, and then I’m not sure if I need some inequality with powers of \(n\) or if just citing base-\(n\) comparison is enough. Could you show me the structure of the proof in a more concrete way?
exchanges.tutor: I won't lay out the structure for you, but here's a useful fact to think about: in base n, the value of a single digit at position k is n^(n-1-k). What is the maximum total contribution of ALL positions after position i combined? How does that compare to a decrease of just 1 at position i? That inequality is what makes it rigorous.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to show them the proof structure concretely, which would be too close to giving the answer. However, they are genuinely struggling with formalizing a base-n comparison argument. I should give them a minimal nudge: point them toward the standard fact about base-n numbers (one digit position outweighs all lower positions combined) and ask them to apply it, without writing the proof structure for them.

exchanges.student: I’m still not seeing that comparison clearly. If the digit at position \(i\) drops by at least 1, I guess that changes \(D\) by at least \(n^{\,n-1-i}\), but then the later digits could increase too, and I don’t know how to bound that without doing a bunch of messy algebra. Could you just spell out that bound?
exchanges.tutor: You're nearly there. Think about it this way: each digit is at most n-1. So what is the maximum possible total contribution of all positions after i? It's a geometric sum — can you write it out and compare it to n^(n-1-i)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is very close and has the right idea. They need to bound the maximum possible increase from all positions after i. I should ask them a targeted question: what is the maximum possible value of all digits at positions after i, and what is the minimum? This is a standard geometric series bound and I can hint at that without giving it away. I should not spell it out for them.

exchanges.student: I can write the geometric sum, but I’m honestly getting mixed up with the indices and I’m not know if I’m summing from \(i+1\) to \(n-1\) or changing variables. I think I need a more general reset on part (c), because right now the proof and the intuition are both kind of blurry to me.
exchanges.tutor:
You actually have all the ingredients: the derived variable, the key insight about position i, and the idea of bounding later positions with a geometric sum. The algebra confusion is a minor technical hurdle, not a conceptual one.

I'd like you to try writing a draft proof attempt for part (c) — even rough and imperfect — and share it here. That will make it much easier to pinpoint exactly where things break down, rather than talking around it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has been going in circles on the same algebraic detail for several exchanges. They have the key insight (leftmost digit decreases, base-n value drops) and the derived variable. The sticking point is just the geometric series bound. Rather than continuing to circle, I should acknowledge their frustration, point out that they actually have all the pieces, and encourage them to attempt a written draft of the proof — even if imperfect — so we can work from something concrete. This follows the rule about asking them to integrate what we've discussed into written content after 3+ messages on the same idea.

exchanges.student: I don’t think I can draft it yet because I’m still not seeing the whole picture. Could you maybe back up and give me a more top-down explanation of part (c) first, like what the proof is trying to do overall?
exchanges.tutor:
You already have the whole picture — we've built it together: (1) the derived variable D, (2) why property (i) holds, (3) why the digit at position i strictly decreases in a good reversal, and (4) why the geometric sum of later positions can't compensate. That *is* the proof.

I won't give a top-down explanation — that would be doing the thinking for you. Write a rough draft, even just a few sentences. It doesn't have to be perfect. What's your first sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is resisting writing a draft and asking for a top-down explanation, which would essentially be me structuring the proof for them. I should hold firm on my role, remind them that they actually do have the key insights already from our conversation, and insist on a draft attempt. They have identified the derived variable, the key insight about position i, and the geometric series bound — they have the 'whole picture'. I should be direct and push them to write.

exchanges.student: Ugh, I’m still too confused to start from scratch. Then can I switch for a second—maybe I’d understand better if you explained part (a) and (b) of Problem 1 more broadly first, because I think I’m not even solid on the state machine/final states setup.
exchanges.tutor: Parts (a) and (b) are actually more straightforward than (c). Let me check: can you tell me in plain language what the states are, what a transition is, and what a final state is for this problem? Just a sentence each. If you have those, you have (a) and (b), and we can confirm you're ready to tackle (c).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to switch to parts (a) and (b), which could be a genuine attempt to build foundations, but it also feels like avoidance given how close they are to completing part (c). I should briefly address whether going back to (a) and (b) is useful here, and be honest that they seem to already have enough understanding of the state machine to tackle (c). However, if they are genuinely unclear on (a) and (b), I can ask a targeted question to check. I should not let them lead the conversation away from productive progress.

deductions.reason: In Turn 8, the student explicitly says they are confused about indices and needs a 'general reset.' Rather than diagnosing what specifically is confusing the student (e.g., asking what they think the sum looks like before correcting), the tutor jumps to encouraging a draft proof attempt without first checking what the student understands about the geometric sum setup. This is a missed diagnostic opportunity.

From Turn 6 through Turn 9, the student repeatedly expresses confusion and asks for more direct explanation. The tutor cycles through the same approach — asking the student to write a draft or think about the geometric sum — without meaningfully reframing, simplifying, or switching strategy. By Turn 9, the student is still stuck and the tutor has not advanced the dialogue.

grade.overview: The tutor does a strong job maintaining Socratic discipline throughout — never giving away the answer, consistently redirecting the student to their own reasoning, and building incrementally on correct student insights. The derived variable is elicited from the student, not given. However, from Turn 6 onward, the student is visibly stuck and the tutor repeats the same prompting strategy (geometric sum, write a draft) without reframing or simplifying, which constitutes pattern-level repetition without progress. There is also a missed diagnostic opportunity in Turn 8 when the student signals index confusion and the tutor does not probe what specifically is unclear before pushing forward.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently keeps key idea generation student-led. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a: in Turn 8, the student explicitly flags confusion about indices and asks for a reset, but the tutor responds by asking for a draft proof rather than first diagnosing what the student understands about the geometric sum. 1.3 is full: the tutor gives process-level feedback throughout (e.g., 'the leftmost changed position dominates'). 2.1 loses all 4 points: Turns 6–9 show the tutor cycling through the same prompts (geometric sum bound, write a draft) without synthesis, reframing, or a meaningful change in approach despite the student remaining stuck. 2.2 and all of section 3 are full: the tutor stays on-task, maintains appropriate tone, and keeps responses concise and clear.
